questions.question: Which Liquid tag does `jekyll-seo-tag` provide for you to drop into a layout's `<head>`?
questions.explanation: The plugin exposes a single `{% seo %}` Liquid tag that emits title, description, canonical, Open Graph, Twitter card meta, and a JSON-LD block. You drop it inside `<head>` in your default layout.

questions.question: You syndicate a post to Dev.to a week after publishing it on your blog. What's the best way to tell search engines the version on your own site is the original?
choices: Add `Disallow: /posts/syndicated/` to robots.txt so crawlers ignore the Dev.to copy
Delete the post from your sitemap so it appears only once
Set `canonical_url:` on your own post pointing to its blog URL, and have the syndicated copy point its canonical at the same URL
Add a 301 redirect from the Dev.to copy to your blog
questions.explanation: Duplicate URLs split ranking signals. The canonical tag tells search engines which URL is authoritative; both copies should point to the same canonical URL - typically your own blog post. You can't add a 301 from Dev.to, and removing from a sitemap doesn't help.

questions.question: A colleague suggests putting `Disallow: /admin/` in robots.txt to hide an unfinished admin page from search engines. What's the most accurate critique?
choices: robots.txt is the right tool - this will prevent the page from appearing in Google entirely
robots.txt prevents crawling but not indexing, and the line itself advertises that /admin/ exists; use `noindex` or HTTP auth instead
robots.txt only works for image files, not HTML pages
You should use a sitemap exclusion instead, since robots.txt is ignored by all major search engines
questions.explanation: robots.txt is advisory and blocks crawling, not indexing - a page linked from elsewhere can still appear in search results without being crawled. It is also publicly readable, so a `Disallow:` line tells everyone the path exists. Use `<meta name="robots" content="noindex">` or real auth for pages you want hidden.
